sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated flow through intravenous catheter models of different internal radius during delivery of a viscous saline substitute to connect a measurable trend with a governing the physical sciences principle. The central principle was that laminar flow through a tube is proportional to the fourth power of radius and inversely proportional to viscosity and length. Before collecting data, the group wrote the relevant relationship as Q = ΔPπr^4/(8ηL), so small radius changes can produce large flow changes. This relationship shaped the hypothesis because it identified which variable should be manipulated, which variables must stay fixed, and what direction the result should move if the interpretation is correct. The investigators designed the study so that the graph could not be interpreted by memorizing a formula name alone; it had to be checked against units, proportionality, and limiting behavior.

The experimental manipulation involved comparing catheters of different radii at the same pressure gradient, length, and fluid viscosity. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: flow increased dramatically with radius rather than changing in simple proportion to radius. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that pressure gradient, catheter length, fluid viscosity, and temperature were controlled. Those controls matter because the same apparent trend could otherwise be explained by a drifting instrument, a changing temperature, an altered sample geometry, or a hidden concentration difference.
Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that flow should double when radius doubles because radius enters linearly. Another was that increasing viscosity should raise flow by pushing more forcefully on the fluid. Both alternatives are tempting because each contains a familiar C/P phrase, but neither accounts for the full pattern when the control variables and the figure are considered together.
The investigators also emphasized that analytical reasoning often turns on mathematical form. Linear, inverse, exponential, logarithmic, and power-law relationships produce different predictions even when they use similar variables. A student therefore has to ask whether doubling a variable doubles the response, halves it, changes it by a square or fourth power, or produces a fractional change.
For this passage, the most useful strategy is to connect the figure to IV catheter radius and flow, preserve the direction of causality, and use the controls to reject explanations based on variables that were not actually changed. The relevant data do not require lengthy arithmetic, but they do require recognizing how the equation, graph, and experimental design support the same conclusion.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the IV catheter radius and flow experiment?

choices.A: Flow should double when radius doubles because radius enters linearly
choices.B: The result follows the fourth-power radius dependence in Poiseuille flow
choices.C: Increasing viscosity should raise flow by pushing more forcefully on the fluid
choices.D: The pattern is best attributed to random measurement noise despite the systematic trend and controls

explanations.A: This choice reflects a plausible misconception, but it does not explain the whole experiment. The claim that Flow should double when radius doubles because radius enters linearly conflicts with the governing relationship in the passage, Q = ΔPπr^4/(8ηL), so small radius changes can produce large flow changes, or with the direction of the figure. The passage reports that flow increased dramatically with radius rather than changing in simple proportion to radius and also states that pressure gradient, catheter length, fluid viscosity, and temperature were controlled. Those controls make it inappropriate to attribute the result to a hidden change in setup, concentration, geometry, temperature, or measurement calibration. The best answer has to account for the manipulated variable and the observed trend together, whereas this option either reverses the expected direction, treats a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuses a controlled variable with the experimental cause.
explanations.B: Correct. This choice fits the manipulation, the graph, and the controls simultaneously. The passage gives the governing relationship as Q = ΔPπr^4/(8ηL), so small radius changes can produce large flow changes, so the relevant reasoning is not just recognizing a topic word but applying the dependency in that relationship. Here, flow increased dramatically with radius rather than changing in simple proportion to radius, while the investigators verified that pressure gradient, catheter length, fluid viscosity, and temperature were controlled. That supports this answer because The result follows the fourth-power radius dependence in Poiseuille flow. It also preserves the direction of the effect and predicts the appropriate follow-up behavior if the manipulated variable were changed again. The other choices usually reverse causality, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, ignore units, or explain the trend by changing a variable that the passage explicitly held constant.
explanations.C: This choice reflects a plausible misconception, but it does not explain the whole experiment. The claim that Increasing viscosity should raise flow by pushing more forcefully on the fluid conflicts with the governing relationship in the passage, Q = ΔPπr^4/(8ηL), so small radius changes can produce large flow changes, or with the direction of the figure. The passage reports that flow increased dramatically with radius rather than changing in simple proportion to radius and also states that pressure gradient, catheter length, fluid viscosity, and temperature were controlled. Those controls make it inappropriate to attribute the result to a hidden change in setup, concentration, geometry, temperature, or measurement calibration. The best answer has to account for the manipulated variable and the observed trend together, whereas this option either reverses the expected direction, treats a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuses a controlled variable with the experimental cause.
explanations.D: This choice reflects a plausible misconception, but it does not explain the whole experiment. The claim that The pattern is best attributed to random measurement noise despite the systematic trend and controls conflicts with the governing relationship in the passage, Q = ΔPπr^4/(8ηL), so small radius changes can produce large flow changes, or with the direction of the figure. The passage reports that flow increased dramatically with radius rather than changing in simple proportion to radius and also states that pressure gradient, catheter length, fluid viscosity, and temperature were controlled. Those controls make it inappropriate to attribute the result to a hidden change in setup, concentration, geometry, temperature, or measurement calibration. The best answer has to account for the manipulated variable and the observed trend together, whereas this option either reverses the expected direction, treats a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuses a controlled variable with the experimental cause.

The passage therefore requires separating the intended independent variable from artifacts that might change the measured signal without reflecting the proposed mechanism.

questions.stem: Which observation from the figure most directly supports the proposed explanation?

choices.B: The figure shows that larger-radius catheters produce disproportionately greater flow under the same driving pressure
choices.C: The measured response changes only after a controlled variable is deliberately altered
choices.D: The plotted trend is opposite to the direction predicted by the stated relationship

choices.B: The same numerical response should occur even if the manipulated variable is reversed

questions.stem: Which broader Chemical and Physical Foundations principle is most directly illustrated by this experiment?

choices.A: Tube radius is a dominant determinant of laminar flow resistance in biological and clinical contexts
choices.B: Quantitative graphs should be interpreted without considering units or controlled variables
choices.C: A controlled variable is the most likely cause of a trend whenever it appears in an equation
choices.D: A memorized definition should override a measured trend when the two appear inconsistent, even though the passage states that pressure gradient was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated a model of alveolar sacs with adjustable surfactant concentration used to measure pressure required to keep small bubbles open to connect a measurable trend with a governing the physical sciences principle. The central principle was that Laplace pressure increases with surface tension and decreases with radius for a spherical interface. Before collecting data, the group wrote the relevant relationship as ΔP = 2T/r, where T is surface tension and r is radius. This relationship shaped the hypothesis because it identified which variable should be manipulated, which variables must stay fixed, and what direction the result should move if the interpretation is correct. The investigators designed the study so that the graph could not be interpreted by memorizing a formula name alone; it had to be checked against units, proportionality, and limiting behavior.

The experimental manipulation involved increasing surfactant concentration while measuring the pressure needed to maintain bubbles of the same radius. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: higher surfactant reduced the pressure required to stabilize the bubble. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that bubble radius, temperature, gas composition, and pressure transducer calibration were controlled. Those controls matter because the same apparent trend could otherwise be explained by a drifting instrument, a changing temperature, an altered sample geometry, or a hidden concentration difference.
Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that surfactant increases surface tension so that alveoli recoil more strongly. Another was that pressure is independent of radius and surface tension in a spherical interface. Both alternatives are tempting because each contains a familiar C/P phrase, but neither accounts for the full pattern when the control variables and the figure are considered together.
The investigators also emphasized that analytical reasoning often turns on mathematical form. Linear, inverse, exponential, logarithmic, and power-law relationships produce different predictions even when they use similar variables. A student therefore has to ask whether doubling a variable doubles the response, halves it, changes it by a square or fourth power, or produces a fractional change.
For this passage, the most useful strategy is to connect the figure to alveolar surface tension pressure, preserve the direction of causality, and use the controls to reject explanations based on variables that were not actually changed. The relevant data do not require lengthy arithmetic, but they do require recognizing how the equation, graph, and experimental design support the same conclusion. The investigators emphasized that the interpretation depends on connecting the measured trend to the relevant physical relationship, the experimental controls, and the limiting behavior of the system rather than selecting a formula by keyword alone.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the alveolar surface tension pressure experiment?

choices.A: Surfactant increases surface tension so that alveoli recoil more strongly
choices.B: Pressure is independent of radius and surface tension in a spherical interface

choices.D: The pressure decrease is best explained by reduced surface tension in the Laplace relationship
